candidates.title: Duplicate submit/retry creates one paid outcome
candidates.why_now: Duplicate charge and duplicate order risks are P0; existing repro harness exercises risky behavior but does not prove exactly one final paid outcome.
candidates.entry_path: WebPublic / Widget / Box Office
candidates.scenario_mix: One paid ticket, normal fees, WebPublic guest checkout, slow submit or replay request, then final one-paid-outcome proof.

evidence_refs.note: Basket updates use idempotency and row locking.
Checkout concurrency docs call out idempotency and locks.
Authorize repro includes panic-click, slow purchase, and replay scenarios.
Diagnostic replay posts the same purchase payload and records replay response evidence.
WebPublic and Widget repro surfaces finish at verifyConfirmation; Box Office uses andVerifyTransaction.
filterByTransactionId can filter Dashboard transactions and expects exactly one actions menu row.
Repro test runs each scenario/surface with environment setup and installed capture hooks.
PostPurchaseStep.result returns CheckoutResult, which can carry invoiceData out of WebPublic/Widget flows.
Payment completion returns invoiceData and verifies confirmation order ID matches invoiceData.transaction_id.

candidates.what_to_automate: Duplicate-submit/retry checkout final-state proof.
candidates.qa_handoff: Build one Playwright test that tries to submit or replay the same checkout purchase twice, then proves the buyer ends with exactly one paid outcome.
candidates.first_action: Default path: use the existing Authorize.net diagnostic-replay-purchase scenario because the replay harness already exists. If the team says Stripe volume should drive this priority, first create or find the same duplicate-submit proof for Stripe and use the same assertions.
candidates.decision_needed: Provider choice only: should the first duplicate-submit proof use the existing Authorize.net replay harness, or should we prioritize a Stripe equivalent because Stripe has higher production volume?
candidates.default_decision: If no provider decision is made, proceed with the existing Authorize.net replay proof because it is the fastest available way to test the duplicate-submit invariant.
acceptance_criteria: The same checkout purchase attempt is submitted or replayed twice.
Dashboard/API shows exactly one successful paid transaction for the captured transaction id.
Exactly one paid order or invoice exists for the buyer/event checkout context.
Issued ticket count matches the requested quantity once, with no duplicate ticket set.
Replay evidence is preserved so a reviewer can see the duplicate attempt happened.
candidates.next_step: Automate a duplicate-submit/retry final-state proof later. Default first implementation is the existing Authorize.net diagnostic-replay-purchase WebPublic guest scenario; switch to a Stripe equivalent first only if provider volume makes Stripe the higher business-priority proof.
candidates.could_change_ranking: Lower or split this ranking if production payment volume makes Stripe the more business-critical first target, or if hidden Qase/API coverage already proves one paid outcome. Keep high if the team wants the fastest duplicate-submit final-state proof using an existing repro harness.
candidates.reader_summary: This is ranked first as the fastest ready duplicate-submit proof because an Authorize.net replay harness already exists. It is not saying Authorize is more important than Stripe. If Stripe carries more checkout volume, a Stripe duplicate-submit/retry proof should be reviewed for promotion.
candidates.recommended_first_test: Create one duplicate-submit proof. Use the existing Authorize.net diagnostic-replay-purchase path by default: send a normal /purchase/ request, replay the same payload, then assert one paid transaction, one order/invoice, and one issued ticket set. If Stripe volume is the business priority, build/find the same proof on Stripe first.
candidates.not_a_matrix: Do not treat this as final provider priority. It is one ready replay proof. If Stripe is the dominant provider, create or promote the equivalent Stripe duplicate-submit/retry final-state proof before finalizing the automation order.
candidates.ranking_basis: Highest current automation-readiness target for duplicate-submit one-paid-outcome proof, not proof that Authorize.net is the highest-volume payment provider.
candidates.production_usage_caveat: If Stripe carries most checkout volume, add or promote a Stripe duplicate-submit/retry final-state candidate and compare it against this Authorize repro target.

candidates.title: Package revenue-realization reporting reconciliation
candidates.why_now: Package checkout has broad purchase automation, but package revenue realization changes reporting and settlement truth through parent/child stat allocation. Inspected Playwright coverage verifies package purchase, confirmation, and Box Office totals, but not child stat allocation or Dashboard/API reporting reconciliation.
candidates.entry_path: Package checkout, preferably one custom package or preset package with child-based revenue realization
candidates.scenario_mix: One package purchase with revenue realization enabled, card payment, then API/Dashboard reporting proves expected parent/child revenue allocation.

evidence_refs.note: Package revenue realization is a two-stage system: stored child allocation percentages, then purchase-time and invoice-time stat propagation.
Stored package percentages can differ from final realized percentages when package value is unavailable or missing at purchase time.
Membership/package realization changes reporting and settlement truth; package allocation percentages flow into child invoice-item stat fields.
Package child group rebuild calls process_revenue_realization_for_groups.
Allocation service sets purchase-time percent_allocation_applied_from_package on child item groups.

content_guidance.audience: QA reviewer deciding what Playwright automation to build next

field_meanings.reader_summary: Plain-English risk summary. This should explain the business risk without implementation jargon.
field_meanings.recommended_first_test: The first representative Playwright proof to build later. This is not a permutation matrix.
field_meanings.not_a_matrix: What the candidate is intentionally not asking us to test first.
field_meanings.scenario_mix: A compact realistic scenario, not a full list of permutations.

content_guidance.writing_rule: Prefer reviewer-readable language over internal shorthand. If a phrase could sound like a matrix request, rewrite it as one representative first test plus an explicit not_a_matrix note.
